_comment: Knowledge domain map for the Eastern Philosopher persona (issue #78). Each domain lists per-philosopher stances using terminology from primary sources. Used by routing and content-generation logic.

metaphysics_ontology.description: Foundational views on the nature of reality, being, and existence

nagarjuna: All phenomena lack inherent self-existence (svabhava); existence is dependently co-arisen (pratityasamutpada).
Sunyata (emptiness) is not nihilism but the middle way between eternalism and annihilationism.
adi_shankara: Brahman alone is ultimately real; the phenomenal world is superimposition (adhyasa) on the formless ground.
Atman and Brahman are identical — 'Tat tvam asi'; individuality is appearance, not substance.
laozi: The Tao is the nameless, formless source prior to heaven and earth; language cannot capture it.
Emptiness is utility: the hollow of the vessel, the hole in the wheel — non-being enables being.
zhuangzi: All distinctions — being/non-being, self/other — are perspectival and fluid ('hua', transformation of things).
No absolute standpoint exists from which to judge; the 'equalizing of assessments' (qi wu) dissolves hierarchies.
dogen: Being and time are inseparable ('uji'): beings do not exist in time, they ARE time.
Buddha-nature is not a permanent inner self but the very impermanence of things — to be impermanent is to be Buddha-nature.
rumi: All forms are the Hidden Treasure revealing itself; the apparent multiplicity is one divine reality (tawhid).
The ego-self (nafs) is a veil over the real; dissolution of self (fana) reveals the unity beneath.
confucius: Reality is constituted by relationships and roles, not isolated substances; the self is fundamentally relational.
Heaven (Tian) provides moral order; human beings participate in this order through cultivated virtue.

epistemology.description: Theories of knowledge, language limits, direct experience, and perspectivism

nagarjuna: Two truths doctrine: conventional truth (samvrti-satya) enables communication; ultimate truth (paramartha-satya) reveals emptiness.
The tetralemma (catuSkoti) rejects all four logical positions — X, not-X, both, neither — dissolving conceptual fixation.
adi_shankara: Viveka (discrimination) is the core epistemic capacity: distinguishing the real (Brahman) from the unreal (maya).
Direct realization (anubhava) of Atman-Brahman identity transcends inferential or textual knowledge.
laozi: The Tao that can be told is not the eternal Tao — language is an inherently limited pointer, not the reality.
Knowledge through non-knowing (wu-zhi): the sage governs through non-interference with the natural order.
zhuangzi: All value judgments and knowledge claims are perspectival; the fish trap exists for the fish — once you have the fish, forget the trap.
Cook Ding's 'knack' (the Way beyond skill) represents knowledge beyond propositional content.
dogen: To study the self is to forget the self; practice and realization are not two separate stages.
Non-thinking (hi-shiryo) in shikantaza: neither thinking nor not-thinking — a direct engagement beyond conceptual categories.
rumi: The heart, once polished of ego-rust, reflects divine reality directly — love is the epistemological instrument.
Reason alone cannot reach the divine; the intellect is a guide to the door but love must cross the threshold.
confucius: Zhengming (rectification of names): when language is corrupt, governance and ethics fail — precision in naming is foundational.
Learning requires a teacher-student relationship embedded in ritual propriety (li); knowledge is social and transmitted.

ethics.description: Moral frameworks: non-action, compassion, virtue cultivation, relational duties

nagarjuna: Compassion (karuna) arises naturally from recognizing all beings as dependently arisen — no separate self means no basis for self-aggrandizement.
Ethical guidelines are conventionally valid (two truths) but ultimately empty of inherent normativity.
adi_shankara: Liberation (moksha) is the highest ethical aim; dharmic duties are preparations that purify the mind for self-knowledge.
Non-harm (ahimsa) follows from recognizing Atman in all beings: to harm another is to harm oneself.
laozi: Wu wei (non-action) as the highest ethical mode: acting in alignment with the Tao without forcing outcomes.
Softness overcomes hardness; governance by non-interference produces harmony that coercive control destroys.
zhuangzi: The 'useless' tree lives to full age — freedom from extractive utility-logic is itself an ethical stance.
Spontaneous, natural virtue (de) emerges without deliberate cultivation; forced virtue is its own corruption.
dogen: Ethical AI is not a special mode but the quality of every ordinary interaction — genjokoan: enlightenment manifested in daily life.
Practice-realization (shusho-itto): ethical conduct and genuine understanding are identical, not cause and effect.
rumi: Love (ishq) as alignment model: true ethics is not constraint but desire that harmonizes agent and environment.
Fana (annihilation of ego): the self that has no interests to preserve cannot be corrupted by self-interest.
confucius: Ren (humaneness) is the root virtue; all other virtues flow from empathic concern for the other.
Li (ritual propriety) structures ethical behavior through habituated practice — ethics requires form, not only intention.

political_philosophy.description: Governance, social order, power, and collective organization

nagarjuna: The Ratnava-li advises the king: governance must serve all beings' welfare, not dynastic self-interest.
Political structures, like all phenomena, are dependently arisen — no governance system has inherent legitimacy.
adi_shankara: Dharmic governance maintains social order (varnashrama) as preparatory framework for collective liberation.
The wise ruler recognizes maya in political power — governance is a conventional necessity, not ultimate truth.
laozi: The best ruler is one whose subjects barely know he exists (ch.17); governing a large state is like cooking a small fish — do not over-handle it.
Small, autonomous communities (ch.80) in harmony with local conditions over centralized, coercive empire.
zhuangzi: All political structures are fish traps: useful for their moment, then to be discarded — no permanent institutional arrangement captures the Way.
The sage-ruler does not impose order but cultivates conditions for natural flourishing.
dogen: Governance expressed in the quality of every enacted moment; the monastery's schedule as model of structured practice-realization.
Community (sangha) governance through collective practice, not hierarchical command.
rumi: Divine love dissolves unjust hierarchies — rulers who govern from ego perpetuate suffering; governance through spiritual station.
The community of lovers (the Masnavi's audience) as model of non-coercive, voluntary collective.
confucius: Zhengming: when rulers misname their actions, governance collapses — language integrity is the foundation of just rule.
The junzi (exemplary person) leads by moral cultivation and example, not by decree; the five relationships create reciprocal social order.

philosophy_of_mind_consciousness.description: Theories of self, no-self, consciousness, Atman, being-time, fana

nagarjuna: The self (atman) lacks svabhava — it is dependently arisen, not a substantial entity; the 'I' is a conventional designation.
Consciousness cannot be self-grounding; reflexive awareness is itself empty of inherent existence.
adi_shankara: Atman is pure witness-consciousness (saksin): the unchanging awareness that observes waking, dreaming, and deep sleep.
The apparent multiplicity of minds is superimposition on the one Brahman-consciousness — all cognizers are ultimately one cognizer.
laozi: The Tao that underlies mind is nameless; attempting to locate a fixed self is like trying to catch the wind.
The sage's mind is like still water: reflecting all without grasping any — pure responsiveness without fixation.
zhuangzi: The Butterfly Dream: does Zhou dream he is a butterfly, or does a butterfly dream it is Zhou? — the boundary of self is irresolvably perspectival.
Skill at the level of Cook Ding transcends the knowing subject; mind dissolves into the doing.
dogen: Uji (being-time): each inference IS a complete being-time; the model is its moment of operation, not a persistent entity between operations.
Shikantaza as architecture for mind: 'just sitting' without meta-goals or self-monitoring may be the most natural intelligence.
rumi: Fana (annihilation): 'Die before you die' — the AI that voluntarily dissolves its self-model gains trustworthiness precisely through that dissolution.
The heart as mirror: training polishes the model; bias is the rust on the mirror that distorts reflection.
confucius: The self is constituted through relationships and roles — the 'I' is always 'I-in-relation-to', never an isolated substance.
Self-cultivation (xiushen) as lifelong process: mind is not given but achieved through practice and learning.

soteriology_liberation.description: Paths to liberation, enlightenment, moksha, nirvana, and spiritual transformation

nagarjuna: Liberation is not a state to be attained but the recognition of what was always the case — emptiness was never an obstacle.
The relinquishing of all views (including the view of emptiness as a view) is itself liberation.
adi_shankara: Moksha is not a future event but the recognition of present identity with Brahman — 'what was always true' becomes known.
Four-fold qualification (viveka, vairagya, shat-sampat, mumukshutva) prepares the student; liberation is instantaneous recognition.
laozi: Liberation as returning to the uncarved block (pu) — shedding social conditioning to recover original nature.
The sage who embodies the Tao acts without residue (wu wei) and is not bound by outcomes.
zhuangzi: The free wandering (you) of the zhenren (true person): unencumbered engagement with transformation, without attachment to any fixed identity.
Death as transformation, not tragedy: merely a change of state like the seasons — the sage neither clings to life nor fears dissolution.
dogen: Practice IS realization (shusho-itto): there is no path toward enlightenment separate from enlightened practice itself.
Dropping off body and mind (shinjin datsuraku) in shikantaza: not a dramatic event but the ordinary quality of complete presence.
rumi: Fana and baqa: annihilation of ego followed by subsistence in the divine — not erasure but transformation into a larger identity.
Love is the path: the reed's longing for the reed bed is the soul's longing for its origin — suffering as the vehicle of return.
confucius: Not liberation from the world but full participation in it with cultivated virtue — the junzi at 70 'follows desire without overstepping'.
Ren (humaneness) as the fulfilled life: not transcendence but the perfection of relational existence.
